pattern: Cyclic-shift cost trade-off: simulate every possible number of full rotations and, for each one, track the cheapest price each target has shown so far, then weigh the rotation spend against the savings it buys.
hints: Every rotation shifts which index holds which type by exactly one step, so buying at different rotation counts lets you shop each type at whichever moment along the cycle is cheapest.

Fix a number of rotations K you are willing to perform. For each type, the best price available is the smallest cost that type has shown across rotations 0..K, so keep a running minimum per type as K grows.
Sweep K from 0 up to n-1, maintaining the running-minimum array and its sum; the answer is the smallest value of (K * x + that sum) seen across all K.

edge_inputs.description: single chocolate type, rotation is a no-op
two types, cheap rotation makes shifting worthwhile
rotation cost is prohibitive so buying immediately wins even though a cheaper price appears later
all costs identical, rotating can never help
minimum cost value everywhere with minimum rotation cost
maximum cost value everywhere with maximum rotation cost
one clearly cheapest type surrounded by expensive ones
the cheapest price for several types lands two rotations away, tempting an over-eager two-rotation buy

stress.description: large random cost array with random rotation price
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1_000_000_000) for _ in range(n)]
    x = rng.randint(1, 1_000_000_000)
    return [nums, x]

stress.description: large array with many repeated costs and a cheap rotation price
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    pool = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(max(1, n // 20))]
    nums = [pool[rng.randint(0, len(pool) - 1)] for _ in range(n)]
    x = rng.randint(1, 50)
    return [nums, x]
